text: The surgeon mobilizes the ascending colon and performs a Kocher maneuver on the duodenum to expose the inferior vena cava (IVC). The lower pole of the kidney is mobilized, and the gonadal vein is identified as it enters the IVC medially. The gonadal vein is ligated and cut to improve maneuverability around the arteriovenous malformation (AVM) sac.

The surgeon performs dissection along the lateral border of the inferior vena cava (IVC) to access the renal hilum. The anterior division of the right renal artery is identified first, followed by the posterior division, which runs alongside a branch of the renal vein. Both divisions are traced to visualize the main trunk of the right renal artery.
The surgeon loops the right renal vein, the anterior division of the right renal artery, and the main trunk of the right renal artery to achieve full vascular control.

The surgeon tightens the loop around the main trunk of the right renal artery, causing the sac to visibly deflate. Upon relaxing the loop, the sac refills. A similar deflation is observed when the loop is tightened around the anterior division of the renal artery.

The surgeon dissects the sac surrounding the anterior division of the renal artery to expose the ostium, as identified in preoperative angiography. Two small aneurysms are visible near the ostium site.

A vascular clamp is applied to the anterior division of the renal artery for vascular control. The single arterial feeder to the arteriovenous malformation (AVM) is identified, clipped, and cut. The margins of the ostia are freshened and repaired using Gore-Tex sutures. After closing the ostia, the vascular clamp is removed to assess hemostasis.

The surgeon dissects the sac from the surrounding parenchyma tissue, causing bleeding to resume. A vascular clamp is reapplied to the main arterial trunk to control the bleeding. The sac is observed draining into a branch of the renal vein, which is then ligated and cut. The vascular clamp is subsequently removed from the main arterial trunk.
The surgical field is irrigated, ensuring hemostasis is achieved. The surgeon performs renorraphy using 3-0 V-Loc sutures, applying them in two layers to repair the renal tissue.
The surgeon reapplies vascular clamps proximally and distally to achieve proper control, then reinforces the small aneurysms near the repaired ostea using a plaget sutured with Gore-Tex suture.
The surgeon removes the vascular clamps and slings, checks hemostasis, applies FlowSeal to the parenchyma region, and sutures a layer of fat over the kidney.
